channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: All eyes on Orion’s heat shield: Artemis 2 astronauts will hit Earth's atmosphere at nearly 24,000 mph on April 10 - Space
item.description:
1. All eyes on Orion’s heat shield: Artemis 2 astronauts will hit Earth's atmosphere at nearly 24,000 mph on April 10  Space
  2. For Artemis II, Returning to Earth May Be the Most Dangerous Part of the Mission  The New York Times
  3. How to watch Artemis II astronauts splash down off San Diego coast  Axios
  4. Artemis II Flight Day 9: Crew Prepares to Come Home  NASA (.gov)
  5. A cracked heat shield rattled NASA after Artemis I. Now, Artemis II will put the fix to the test  Los Angeles Times

item.title: Scientists say the world’s oldest octopus fossil isn’t an octopus after all - AP News
item.description:
1. Scientists say the world’s oldest octopus fossil isn’t an octopus after all  AP News
  2. The ‘oldest octopus’ in the world isn’t an octopus after all, scientists find  Yahoo News New Zealand
  3. "Weird blob" creature thought to be the world's oldest octopus isn't an octopus after all. Here's what scientists found.  CBS News
  4. This Fossil Once Rewrote Everything We Knew About Octopuses. Turns Out, It's Not An Octopus At All  IFLScience
  5. 'Oldest octopus' fossil is no octopus at all, scans reveal  Phys.org

item.title: 'We all pretty much broke down right there': Inside the Artemis 2 astronauts' emotional moment near the moon - Space
item.description:

item.title: A 289-Million-Year-Old Mummified Reptile From An Oklahoma Cave Shows How We Breathe The Way We Do - IFLScience
item.description:
1. A 289-Million-Year-Old Mummified Reptile From An Oklahoma Cave Shows How We Breathe The Way We Do  IFLScience

  3. The oldest breath: A 300-million-year-old mummy reveals the origins of how amniotes breathe  EurekAlert!
  4. Permian Fossil is Earliest Evidence of Rib-Powered Breathing  Sci.News

item.title: One DNA letter can trigger complete sex reversal - Phys.org
item.description:
1. One DNA letter can trigger complete sex reversal  Phys.org

item.description:

item.title: This Company Is Launching 4,000 Orbiting ‘Sky Mirrors’ to Beam Sunlight Down to Earth After Dark. Scientists Want It Stopped Now - Indian Defence Review
item.description:

item.title: Satellite images reveal hidden cause of melting glaciers in Greenland - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description:
